does: This paper presents RefusalBench, a generative evaluation framework for assessing whether language models in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems can selectively refuse to answer questions when provided with flawed or unreliable context. Rather than relying on static benchmarks, the framework programmatically generates diagnostic test cases through 176 distinct linguistic perturbation strategies organized across six categories of informational uncertainty at three intensity levels. The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study evaluating over 30 models, revealing that even frontier models achieve below 50% refusal accuracy on multi-document tasks and exhibit systematic failure modes including dangerous overconfidence or excessive caution. The study decomposes selective refusal into separable detection and categorization skills, and demonstrates that this capability is trainable and alignment-sensitive, independent of model scale or extended reasoning abilities.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in language model safety evaluation at the intersection of RAG systems and refusal capabilities. It builds on prior work in hallucination detection and grounded generation that has identified failures when models generate confident answers despite unreliable sources, extending these insights into the underexplored domain of selective refusal in context-dependent settings. The paper responds to limitations of static evaluation benchmarks, which earlier studies have shown models can exploit through memorization and dataset artifacts. RefusalBench advances generative evaluation methodology, similar to recent approaches in adversarial robustness testing, by using programmatic perturbation to create controlled, diverse test cases rather than relying on hand-crafted examples. The framework also connects to broader work on alignment and safety training, building on methods for steering model behavior through fine-tuning while evaluating whether capabilities generalize.
enables: This work establishes selective refusal as a measurable, improvable capability, opening multiple research directions. The decomposition of refusal into detection and categorization skills provides a structured foundation for targeted training interventions and could guide development of specialized denoising or uncertainty estimation techniques for RAG systems. The generative perturbation framework itself can be extended to other safety-critical evaluation domains beyond refusal, providing a methodological blueprint for creating robust, artifact-free benchmarks. The finding that selective refusal is alignment-sensitive but scale-invariant suggests novel training approaches may be more efficient than simply scaling model size. Future work can leverage RefusalBench to develop and evaluate improved refusal mechanisms, explore how to make models robust across perturbation strategies, and investigate whether selective refusal skills transfer across domains or remain task-specific. The framework also enables longitudinal tracking of model safety improvements as systems evolve.
